name: web-cli-teleport
description: Guide users on when to use Claude Code Web vs CLI and seamlessly teleport sessions between environments
capabilities: Analyzes task complexity, iteration needs, and user location to recommend optimal interface
Provides decision matrix comparing Web and CLI capabilities, strengths, and limitations
Guides users through session teleportation protocol from Web to CLI
Presents workflow patterns for mobile planning to desktop implementation
Offers best practices for context preservation and interface switching
author: DNYoussef
securitySummary: Pure documentation skill containing only markdown guidance and a Graphviz flowchart. No executable code, scripts, network calls, or file system modifications. Static findings are all false positives: hash strings misinterpreted as cryptographic algorithms, documentation backticks as shell execution, and task analysis text as system reconnaissance.
valueStatement: Users often struggle to decide between Claude Code Web and CLI interfaces. This skill analyzes task complexity, location, and requirements to recommend the best interface and guides seamless session teleportation when needs change.
limitations: Does not execute commands or modify files - provides guidance only
Cannot perform actual teleportation - user must run teleport command manually
Does not integrate with external tools or APIs

useCases.title: Plan on mobile, implement on desktop
useCases.description: Use Claude Code iOS app to plan during commute, then teleport to CLI for implementation

useCases.title: Quick web fixes with CLI complexity
useCases.description: Start simple tasks on web for quick wins, teleport to CLI when complexity increases

useCases.title: Collaborative review workflow
useCases.description: Initial work on CLI, create PR, team reviews on web, teleport back for revisions

promptTemplates.title: Quick interface question
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to [task description]. Should I use Claude Code Web or CLI?

promptTemplates.title: Task analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Use web-cli-teleport skill to plan approach for implementing [feature] with [requirements]

promptTemplates.title: Mid-task switch
promptTemplates.prompt: I started this on Claude Code Web but it is getting complex. Help me teleport to CLI and continue.

promptTemplates.title: Mobile workflow
promptTemplates.prompt: I am on mobile and need to plan [task]. How can I use Claude Code Web to get started and teleport later?

bestPractices: Start simple tasks on web for quick wins, teleport when complexity increases
Always clean git state before teleporting to avoid conflicts
Check context percentage after teleporting to verify full history loaded
antiPatterns: Struggling with CLI limitations on web instead of teleporting early
Teleporting with dirty git state causing conflicts
Starting complex iterative tasks on web when CLI inline diffs would help

faq.question: What is the difference between Claude Code Web and CLI?
faq.answer: Web offers no local setup, works on mobile, includes GitHub PR creation. CLI provides inline diffs, local testing, full file access, and VS Code integration.

faq.question: How does session teleportation preserve context?
faq.answer: The teleport command loads approximately 50 percent of the conversation history, preserving context and previous decisions without re-explaining requirements.

faq.question: Can I teleport from CLI back to Web?
faq.answer: Yes, you can switch back to web for final PR review. Some workflows involve multiple switches between interfaces as task complexity changes.

faq.question: What blocks a successful teleport?
faq.answer: Dirty git state is the main blocker. Stash or commit local changes before running the teleport command to avoid conflicts.

faq.question: Is my data safe during teleportation?
faq.answer: Yes. This skill only provides guidance. Session teleportation is a built-in Claude Code feature that securely transfers conversation context.

faq.question: When should I use Claude Code Web over CLI?
faq.answer: Use Web for simple one-off tasks, mobile access, quick fixes, PR creation, and collaborative review. Use CLI for complex debugging, large refactoring, and local testing.
